source: kb/Engineering + CTO Office/2024-11-28_Olympus SaaS Check-in - Monthly (5).txt
content:
- Olympus SaaS Check-in - Monthly Office Hours – Structured Summary for Ramki Gaddipati’s Digital Twin

II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call:

- This was a comprehensive check-in on the Olympus SaaS program’s monthly releases, feature deliverables, and forward roadmap, attended by heads of engineering, SRE, product, and program management. The agenda included review of recent release items, current progress on modules (notably egress, Atropos, and Cipher), process improvements (JIRA scoping, documentation alignment), live blockers (AWS console access, migration integration, SLO/SLA deficiencies), and quality/predictability metrics. Ramki’s focus was on clarifying module and team accountability, enforcing architectural and security best practices (SRE home, AWS console), sharpening deliverable prioritization (egress, anomaly detection, db/cost optimizations), and setting explicit expectations on quality of service, observability, and cost efficiency. Several contextual clarifications, instructions, and escalations for design or future focus were issued as discussed items unfolded.

IV. Key Takeaways from the Call:

- Explicit Module/Team Accountability Required: Ramki emphasized clear mapping between SaaS modules and responsible teams for each deliverable to improve traceability and ownership. (e.g., “add another column called teams matters gets sort”)
- AWS Console Access Must Be Restricted: Direction to strictly limit AWS console access via SRE home with explicit authorization and audit, to minimize risk and ensure compliance. (access “should have explicit authorization”, “do not give access to everybody”)
- SRE Home Is the Single Gateway: All SRE actions and launches must be routable via SRE Home portal—no direct, unmanaged console access. (e.g., “feature is not complete until it is there on SRE home”)
- Egress Gateway Architecture to Be Presented: Request for clear walk-through of egress gateway (HTTPS, route handling) by responsible team, signaling priority on network/egress maturation.
- Jeeves Productionization and Anomaly Detection Priority: Jeeves framework to be made production-grade, with anomaly detection (especially on flow metrics) to be integrated and visible for alerts—highlighting operational maturity.
- Prioritize Cost Reduction in Cipher: Ramki redirected effort to immediate, tangible cost savings, mandating work towards lowering Cipher database storage and demonstrating zero-db architectures save costs, with potential budget reallocation benefits.
- Atropos Toolkit, SLOs, and Quality of Service Outstanding: Stressed defining, tracking, and transparently monitoring Atropos SLOs/SLAs as a critical blocker, noting lack of clarity and urgency around service guarantees and systemic end-to-end assurance (“I want to know how you are prepared to deliver the requisite assurances”).
- Monitoring Philosophy—End-to-End Guarantee: Insistence that monitoring/observability isn’t about components in isolation, but about the real end-user and system outcomes; incomplete SLO thinking is called out as a fundamental issue.
- Publisher Home and Release Drift: Noted slippage in Publisher Home migration/integration cycles, seeking escalation for timely completion and clarity on causes.
- Change Management & Documentation Gaps: Direction to align planning, roadmap, and documentation to PCMM format, flagging persistent gaps in being systematic, and instructing to iterate towards standardization.
- Decision to Drop/Defer Some Features: Select features (e.g., “EI vault”, certain event publishing controls) are intentionally deprioritized in favor of high-leverage items, per Ramki’s prioritization.

V. Ramki's Insights:

1. On Module Accountability and Mapping: Ramki spoke with clarity and insistence, pushing for operational transparency on who is accountable for which SaaS module in all tracking and reporting. He challenged the group to fix ambiguous ownership—wanting records that clearly map modules to teams, reducing confusion and improving future retrospection and governance.

2. On SRE Home and Access Controls: Using firm, directive language, Ramki set a non-negotiable policy: AWS console access should only occur via structured paths (SRE Home), with group-based, explicitly authorized (not role-based) access, and regular review/audits. He underscored the importance for future scalability and compliance, especially as cloud providers may shift (e.g., transitioning to Azure).

3. Architectural Integration and End-to-End Flows: Ramki’s interjections around egress, SRE Home, and features like the onboarding of Teleport, highlighted his insistence on holistic integration. He repeatedly pointed out that a feature’s integration isn’t complete unless tightly bound into centralized, auditable, and secure workflows, with all critical access and launches going through expected automation/gateways.

4. Monitoring, SLOs, and Deliverable Philosophy: Throughout the discussion, Ramki critiqued any thinking that reduces quality or monitoring to technical component coverage. He insisted that SLOs/SLAs be defined from the perspective of actual business/customer outcomes, not just system health, and rejected “monitoring without SLOs” as superficial. He used analogies (“shipping a car vs shipping components”) to clarify that partial solutions do not constitute a coherent platform deliverable.

5. Production Readiness, Cost-Effectiveness, and Pragmatic Prioritization: Ramki forcefully redirected efforts to ensure activities yield real value—explicitly calling to cut database storage costs in Cipher, highlighting “easy wins” for budget optimization, and deprioritizing non-immediate features. His argument for operational efficiency was framed as both a business and engineering imperative.

6. Empowerment But Demand for Discipline: He encouraged teams to pursue cost-saving initiatives that directly add to the platform’s value proposition (“buy the Olympus build budget with the reduced run cost”), but with the expectation of justified business cases and systematic, ongoing review.

VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items:

1. Egress Gateway Architecture Walkthrough
- Task: Present the current egress gateway (esp. HTTPS handling and route mapping), including design, deployment context, and performance implications.
- Assigned To: Venkata Praveen Irrinki / Relevant platform engineering team.
- Ramki's Instructions: Prepare a concise session; be ready to explain both architectural/technical choices (how HTTPS requests, routing are executed) and cloud integration, as Ramki expects detailed, incremental change plans and explicit demonstration of readiness for sensitive rollout.
- Timeline Mentioned: Schedule a dedicated session as soon as possible; no explicit deadline but urgency indicated.

2. Restrict AWS Console Access, Migrate to SRE Home
- Task: Lock down AWS console access, ensuring only explicitly authorized SREs (via a moderated AD group) can access, with all required actions to be routed through SRE Home.
- Assigned To: SRE/Cloud Infrastructure team (“run” side, as per context), with Ramesh Javvaji and Manas Ray actioning checks.
- Ramki's Instructions: Explicitly audit current access, revoke role/title-based grants, ensure all new grants are on explicit approval; migrate SRE tools/actions so no one needs direct AWS console access except by exception.
- Timeline Mentioned: Expectation of immediate review and action initialization.

3. Advance Productionization of Jeeves (Alert Automation Framework)
- Task: Move the Jeeves automation from initial framework to a productionized state; use case—automated cookbook execution for SRE alerts.
- Assigned To: Srikanth Bringu’s team (Jeeves, SRE Platform).
- Ramki's Instructions: Focus on the cookbook use-case for SREs first; ensure operational integration is seamless for handling real alert flows; coordinate with interns if required to expedite the anomaly detection for flow metrics.
- Timeline Mentioned: Target for this quarter (implied urgency).

4. Implement Anomaly Detection for Flow Metrics
- Task: Enable anomaly detection for flow metrics, especially as part of the monitoring/alerting suite.
- Assigned To: SRE/Observability team (Mohit Srivastava to verify ownership).
- Ramki's Instructions: Do not over-engineer—start with basic flow metrics, avoid unnecessary generalization; make anomalies visible in all visualizations/alerting paths.
- Timeline Mentioned: Should be actionable in the short term, given described as a “very small thing.”

5. Define SLOs/SLAs for Atropos and Scheduled Events
- Task: Formally articulate SLOs and SLAs for the Atropos toolkit/platform and all scheduled event services; ensure service monitoring starts from these guarantees.
- Assigned To: Aleem Javed (Atropos), coordinated with Ramesh Javvaji, Mohit Srivastava, and product teams.
- Ramki's Instructions: Clarity, precision, and alignment to customer/business expectations required; must review current gaps and collaborate to close them; scheduled event monitoring flagged as critical due to business impact (e.g., payment due reminders).
- Timeline Mentioned: Implied immediate initiation, but acceptance that it may take “two or three months” for systematization.

6. Drive Cipher Cost Reduction and Database Auditing
- Task: Prioritize work on reducing Cipher database footprint and cost; review all databases in Olympus for consolidation and cost optimization.
- Assigned To: Ramesh Javvaji, Run/DB teams, with cross-team input.
- Ramki's Instructions: Demonstrate pathway to minimal database storage for Cipher (only credentials/auth needed), audit usage, and identify million-dollar cost saves; link budget reductions to Olympus build justifications.
- Timeline Mentioned: Immediate focus for upcoming planning cycles.

VII. Jira Ticket:

- Scope: Restrict AWS Console Access and Migrate all SRE actions to SRE Home Portal.
- Assignee: SRE/Cloud Platform team (Ramesh Javvaji/Manas Ray with SRE group)
- Justification: Critical for security, compliance, and operational standardization as directly mandated by Ramki, with expectation of imminent action and audit tractability.

- Scope: Productionize Jeeves for Alert Automation & Cookbook Integration.
- Assignee: Srikanth Bringu/SRE Automation
- Justification: Required to elevate SRE response and platform maturity; quarter delivery commitment per Ramki’s view; limited scope as described.

- Scope: Define and Publish SLOs/SLAs for Atropos and Scheduled Events.
- Assignee: Aleem Javed, Mohit Srivastava, Ramesh Javvaji (product/ops coordination)
- Justification: Unblocked on clarity around service quality and readiness; systemic gap noted as a core platform risk.

- Scope: Implement Anomaly Detection on Flow Metrics (alerting/dashboard integration).
- Assignee: SRE Observability/Monitoring team (Mohit Srivastava/others)
- Justification: Straightforward, bounded deliverable; called “very small” by Ramki, enabling quick wins.

- Scope: Execute Cipher DB Audit and Cost Reduction Plan.
- Assignee: Run/DB Team; Ramesh Javvaji (lead)
- Justification: High leverage, directly drives platform cost and business justification; per Ramki, links to budget and value realignment.

- Note: Not all action items are suitable for Jira—many involve planning, design decisions, or ongoing routines. For instance, feature deprioritization, process changes toward PCMM documentation, or roadmap prioritization discussions should be handled outside transactional Jira tasks.

VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions:

- Request a focused, dedicated review meeting to walk Ramki through the latest egress gateway deployment, with detailed breakdown of security and routing.
- Recommend periodic (perhaps quarterly) audits of all privileged cloud access permissions, to ensure ongoing compliance with Ramki’s directive.
- Advise product/ops teams to immediately begin compiling and documenting SLO/SLA proposals for all Olympus modules, prioritizing customer-impacting services like Atropos and scheduled events.
- Suggest rapid prototyping/demos of anomaly detection on live flow metrics to build confidence and tune business value before system-wide rollout.
- Encourage cost-optimization workshops across teams to systematically surface and validate additional potential for platform savings, extending beyond Cipher where feasible.

- This summary structures Ramki’s direct inputs, strategic intent, and instructions in context for his Digital Twin, supporting both operational recall and decision-context modeling.
title: 2024-11-28_Olympus SaaS Check-in - Monthly (5)
